title: The practice of being alone without it meaning you are abandoned
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The hollow ache of being alone

I remember the first time I sat in my apartment after leaving a relationship that wasn't safe. The silence was so loud it hurt my chest. Every creak of the floorboards felt like a verdict. Every empty chair was a witness to my failure. I wasn't just alone. I was abandoned. That's what my nervous system told me. And it wasn't lying.

Here's the thing. When you grow up in an environment where connection meant survival, being alone triggers the same circuits as being left for dead. Your body doesn't know the difference between solitude and exile. It just knows that the signal of safety (another person) is gone. And it sounds the alarm. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades mapping how the body holds trauma. He talks about how we freeze, flee, or fawn when danger appears. But what happens when the danger is the absence of people? What happens when your own company feels like a threat?

I've been there. You've been there. You're probably there right now, reading this in a room that feels too quiet, with a phone in your hand that doesn't buzz, with a calendar that has too many empty squares. And I need you to hear this: that ache is real. It's not weakness. It's not neediness. It's your nervous system doing exactly what it was designed to do. It's trying to keep you alive by finding connection. The problem is, it hasn't learned that you can survive alone.

What your nervous system actually needs

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, gave us a framework that changed how I understand this. He talks about three states: ventral vagal (safe and social), sympathetic (fight or flight), and dorsal vagal (freeze and collapse). When you're alone and your body interprets that as abandonment, you're likely in a sympathetic state. Your heart races. Your thoughts spiral. You feel restless and panicked. Or you drop into dorsal. You feel numb, heavy, disconnected from your own body. You might dissociate. You might sleep too much. You might just stare at the wall for hours.

Neither state is wrong. They're both survival strategies. But here's what I've learned from my own recovery and from studying this work: the practice of being alone without it meaning abandonment is not about forcing yourself to feel okay. It's not about toxic positivity or telling yourself "I'm fine" when you're not. It's about teaching your nervous system, slowly and gently, that solitude can be safe. That you can be your own anchor.

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

Think about it. When a baby cries, they need someone to come. That's wired into us. But as adults, we have the capacity to become that someone for ourselves. It doesn't happen overnight. It doesn't happen because you read a blog post. It happens because you practice. You sit with the discomfort. You notice what your body does. You breathe. You stay. And eventually, your system learns that the person who was supposed to come, the person who never came when you were small, that person can be you.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana, who studied directly with Porges, wrote it. It's full of exercises that help you map your own nervous system states and find small ways to shift toward safety. I recommend it because it doesn't ask you to pretend you're fine. It asks you to get curious about what's actually happening.

The difference between isolation and solitude

Here's a distinction that saved my life. Isolation is when you feel cut off from connection, even if you're in a crowd. Solitude is when you choose to be alone and feel a sense of peace. The difference isn't about how many people are around. It's about your internal state. It's about whether your nervous system registers safety or threat.

When I first started trying to be alone intentionally, I couldn't tell the difference. I would sit on my couch and my mind would immediately go to the past. The faces of people who left. The voices of people who hurt me. The stories I told myself about why I was unlovable. That wasn't solitude. That was being haunted. That was my dorsal vagal system pulling me into a freeze state where I replayed old wounds.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who worked with Bessel van der Kolk, talks about how trauma survivors often experience time as nonlinear. The past doesn't stay in the past. It lives in your body. So when you're alone, you're not just alone in the present. You're alone with every moment you ever felt abandoned. Every time your parent didn't show up. Every time your partner walked away. Every time you were left to fend for yourself before you had the tools to do it.

That's a heavy load. And I don't want you to think you can just shake it off. You can't. But you can start to separate the present moment from the past. You can start to notice: right now, in this room, am I actually in danger? Or am I just remembering danger? That's the practice. And it's not easy. But it's possible.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She was one of the first to articulate that trauma is not the event itself but the way the event lives in the nervous system. Reading her work helped me understand that my inability to be alone wasn't a character flaw. It was a symptom of a system that had been overwhelmed. That knowledge alone, that reframe, was the beginning of something.

Building the muscle of self-presence

I want to be honest with you. I still struggle with this. I still have days where being alone feels like drowning. Where I reach for my phone, scroll through apps, text people I don't even want to talk to, just to feel less empty. Those days are not failures. They're data. They tell me where my nervous system is at. And they give me a chance to practice something different.

What does that practice look like? For me, it starts with the body. I notice the sensations. The tightness in my chest. The knot in my stomach. The urge to move, to escape, to distract. I don't try to change them. I just name them. "Oh, there's that familiar feeling of being left." Then I put my hand on my chest. I take a slow breath. I say to myself, "I'm here. I'm not going anywhere."

Let me be honest with you about something. Recovery is expensive. Not just financially - though therapy and bodywork and supplements and books add up fast. It's expensive in time, in energy, in relationships. Some people won't understand why you're changing. Some people benefited from your old patterns and they won't like the new ones. That's real. And it's painful.

But here's what's also real. The cost of not healing is higher. It's paid in health - in autoimmune conditions, in chronic pain, in shortened lifespan. It's paid in relationships - in repeating the same patterns, in choosing the same unavailable partners, in pushing away the people who could actually love you. It's paid in aliveness - in years spent numb, disconnected, going through the motions.

I'm not saying this to scare you. I'm saying it because the choice to heal isn't a luxury. It's a necessity. And it's one that only you can make. No one can do this work for you. But you don't have to do it alone. That's the paradox of recovery - it's deeply personal work that requires other people. You need witnesses. You need co-regulators. You need at least one person who can see you clearly and not look away. Find that person. Or let them find you.

It sounds simple. It's not. The first hundred times you do this, it might feel fake. It might feel like you're lying to yourself. That's okay. Your nervous system doesn't trust words yet. It trusts repetition. It trusts presence. It trusts the fact that you keep showing up, even when it feels pointless. Wild, right? The thing that heals you is the thing you want to run from the most. Staying.

I've found that having something tangible to hold or focus on helps. A warm cup of tea. A blanket with a specific texture. A book that speaks to the experience. A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She writes about the RAIN practice (Recognize, Allow, Investigate, Nurture) which is basically a way to be with your difficult emotions without trying to fix them. It's not about making the aloneness go away. It's about learning to be with it.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises that help you track your triggers and build self-compassion. I used it on nights when I couldn't stop the spiral. It gave me something to do with my hands and my mind that wasn't just suffering.

What happens when you stay

I can't promise you that this practice will make the loneliness disappear. That's not the point. The point is that you learn to tolerate it. And then, slowly, you learn to be curious about it. And then, one day, you might find that being alone doesn't feel like abandonment anymore. It might feel like a choice. It might feel like freedom.

I remember a night about two years into my recovery. I was sitting on my porch, alone, watching the stars. And I realized I wasn't waiting for anyone. I wasn't hoping someone would call. I wasn't replaying old conversations. I was just there, with myself, and it was okay. Not great. Not transcendent. Just okay. And that felt like a miracle.

Sit with that. The miracle isn't that you feel amazing alone. The miracle is that you can be alone and not feel like you're dying. That's the goal. That's the return. You return to yourself, not as a project to be fixed, but as a presence to be kept company.

Your nervous system learned to associate aloneness with danger. That was adaptive once. It kept you alert, kept you looking for connection when connection was necessary for survival. But you're not that small anymore. You have resources now. You have a body that can feel, a mind that can notice, a heart that can hold its own pain. You have the capacity to be the person you needed when you were little. The person who stays. The person who doesn't leave.

That's the practice of being alone without it meaning you are abandoned. It's not a destination. It's a daily, sometimes hourly, choice. You choose to stay. You choose to breathe. You choose to notice that you are here, right now, and that is enough. It might not feel like enough. But it is. I promise you, it is.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical
